one_line_takeaway: A broad 2019-2023 survey that taxonomizes deep-learning IDS work into eight algorithm families and catalogs their datasets/attacks, but runs no experiments of its own and reports surveyed papers' accuracies without cross-comparison.
key_finding: The survey concludes that deep-learning IDS approaches generally outperform shallow/traditional ML (SVM, KNN, RF, LR, NB, DT) on the common benchmarks, that anomaly-based deep models handle previously unseen attacks better than signature-based ones, and that research has increasingly targeted IoT, cloud, SDN, and vehicular environments. NSL-KDD, KDDCup99/KDD99, and UNSW-NB15 dominate as evaluation datasets despite being dated, while newer IoT-oriented datasets (BoT-IoT, IoTID20, CICIDS2017, CSE-CIC-IDS2018, MQTT-IoT-IDS2020, IoT-23, LITNET-2020, NetML-2020) are gaining use. Reported accuracies of surveyed systems span a very wide range (e.g., 87% up to 99.999%), which the review lists but does not critically normalize.
core_contribution: A systematic literature review (covering 2019-2023) of deep-learning algorithms applied to network Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS). The paper's OWN contribution is a taxonomy and narrative synthesis: it groups roughly 30+ surveyed works into eight deep-learning families (CNN, Deep Belief Networks, Autoencoders, DNN, RNN/LSTM/GRU, Self-Normalizing Neural Networks, MLP, and hybrid models) and consolidates their datasets and attack coverage into two summary tables (Table 1: algorithm/problem/method; Table 2: dataset/attacks). It answers three stated research questions: which DL methods have been used in IDS in the last five years, their application areas, and which attacks are covered by the datasets used. The authors propose NO new IDS, model, or experiment of their own; all architectures, accuracies, and results discussed belong to the cited primary studies, not to this review.
threat_model: The reviewed domain concerns network intrusions detected by NIDS/HIDS. Across the surveyed datasets and papers the threat classes include DoS/DDoS, Probe/Reconnaissance, R2L, U2R, botnet, exploits, backdoors, fuzzers, worms, shellcode, generic attacks, Man-in-the-Middle, wormhole/blackhole/sinkhole (IoT), Mirai-style flooding, and vehicular CAN-bus attacks (fuzzy, spoof). Detection is framed as signature-based vs. anomaly-based, with anomaly detection favored for novel attacks. NOTE: the review itself is a survey and does not defend any system or present its own threat model; the above is the aggregate threat surface of the works it summarizes.
limitations: As a review: (1) it is a narrative synthesis without a documented reproducible search protocol, inclusion/exclusion criteria, or quantitative meta-analysis; (2) it reports each surveyed paper's self-reported accuracy uncritically and on heterogeneous train/test splits, so the numbers are not comparable across works and no head-to-head re-evaluation is done; (3) heavy reliance of the surveyed corpus on outdated datasets (KDD99/NSL-KDD) is noted only lightly; (4) several table/text entries show transcription inconsistencies (e.g., 'UNSW-NB18' where UNSW-NB15 is meant, garbled citation markers like 'citation28', and 'implementation method not clear' for ref [35]); (5) the conclusion/future-work section (Section 4) is not substantively developed in the extracted body. The review contributes organization and breadth rather than new empirical evidence.

datasets_used: KDD99 / KDDCup99 (discussed in surveyed papers; DoS, Probe, R2L, U2R)
NSL-KDD (most frequently used benchmark across surveyed papers)
UNSW-NB15 (Analysis, Backdoor, DoS, Exploits, Fuzzers, Generic, Recon, Shellcode, Worms)
CSI (Channel State Information) data, Car hack 2020
quantitative_results: Hu et al. (CNN with CSI, physical-layer Wi-Fi sensing): avg detection accuracy 98.69% single person, 98.91% multiple participants
Syariful et al. (DNN on UNSW Bot-IoT, 75% training split): avg detection accuracy 99.999% (with noted overfitting via small validation loss)
Khan et al. (DNN on MQTT-IoT-IDS2020): 99% accuracy binary, 98% accuracy multiclass
Louati & Ktata (DL-MFID, MLP+KNN multi-agent on KDD Cup 99): 99.95% accuracy
Abdulrahman et al. (MLP1 on NSL-KDD): 95.6% accuracy; MLP2 (two hidden layers) 2.2% higher
Alkahtani & Aldhyani (IoTID20 with PSO): CNN 96.60%, LSTM 99.82%, CNN-LSTM 98.80% accuracy
Ashiku & Dagli (CNN + regularized MLP on UNSW-NB15): 95.4% (pre-partitioned) and 95.6% (user-defined) multiclass accuracy
Amutha et al. (NID-RNN with LSTM on NSL-KDD): 99.4% accuracy, an 8% improvement over other DL algorithms
Sunil et al. (Bidirectional RNN, LSTM+GRU on CICIDS2017): 99.13% classification accuracy using only 58% of dataset attributes

Kasongo (XGBoost feature selection + RNNs): XGBoost-LSTM NSL-KDD binary test acc 88.13% (val 99.49%, train time 225.46s); XGBoost-Simple-RNN UNSW-NB15 test acc 87.07%; XGBoost-LSTM NSL-KDD multiclass 86.93%; XGBoost-GRU UNSW-NB15 multiclass 78.40%
Ashwaq et al. (RNN on NSL-KDD): 87% accuracy
Gulia et al. (G-ABC feature selection + DNN, cloud): 96% detection rate
Muhuri et al. (LSTM-RNN + Genetic Algorithm on NSL-KDD): GA reduced features from 122 to 99
baselines_compared: N/A for the review itself: this is a survey that performs no experiments and therefore reports no baselines of its own.

Baselines used WITHIN the surveyed primary studies include: SVM, KNN, Random Forest, Logistic Regression, Naive Bayes, Decision Tree, AdaBoost, Deep Belief Networks, PCA-DNN, standalone MLP, Stacked Auto-Encoder (SAE), Non-symmetric Deep Auto-Encoder (NDAE), Inverse Weight Clustering, and CASCADE-ANN.
